title: Needle-felted red kite
subtitle: A broad-winged raptor in rust, cream, and grey
excerpt: Build a 25 cm wingspan red kite with a wire armature supporting the forked tail and outstretched wings. Reddish-brown body, pale grey head, and cream-streaked underparts are worked in layered wool tops. An advanced project requiring patience and careful wire handling.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from UK guild armature-bird construction techniques and published advanced needle-felting guides. Wire-supported wing panels on felt backing are the standard approach for large birds with outstretched poses. No single public domain text.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed wool fibres aligned in parallel. Smoother and finer than roving; good for surface detail and colour work.
Fine-stapled wool from Merino sheep. Felts readily and produces a smooth, dense surface.
An internal wire skeleton that gives a needle-felted figure its pose and prevents floppy or drooping sections.
The tiny notch cut into a felting needle shaft. Barbs catch wool fibres and push them together with each stabbing stroke.
Wool fibre drawn into a long, loosely twisted ribbon, ready for spinning or felting.

content.text: The red kite is a large, elegant raptor distinguished by its forked tail, broad wings, and reddish-brown plumage. At a 25 cm wingspan this is a substantial needle-felted piece and one of the most technically demanding birds you can build. The key challenge is the
armature
: the wing panels must be supported by galvanised wire wrapped in
roving
before felting begins. Plan to spread this project across multiple sessions.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

attrs.title: Wire handling
attrs.body: Galvanised wire ends are sharp. Bend all cut ends back on themselves with pliers before wrapping in roving.

content.text: Body core: 80 g reddish-brown
merino
roving. Surface colours:
wool tops
in rust, cream, pale grey, dark brown, and black.
Armature
: 0.9 mm galvanised wire. Wing panels: one white or off-white pre-felted sheet, 30 cm x 15 cm. You will also need craft pliers.
Method

Step 1: wire armature. Cut a 40 cm length of 0.9 mm galvanised wire. Bend it into a T-shape with the crossbar 25 cm wide (the wingspan) and the vertical 15 cm long (the body axis). Bend each end back 1 cm with pliers so no sharp points are exposed. Wrap the entire wire frame tightly with thin wisps of reddish-brown
roving
, overlapping each wrap slightly.

Step 2: body core. Build the body mass along the vertical wire by wrapping layers of reddish-brown merino roving and felting in with a 36-gauge needle directly against the wire-wrapped core. Work from front to back, building a teardrop shape roughly 10 cm long and 4 cm wide at the chest. Do not worry about the surface colour at this stage; you are building structure.

Step 3: head. Roll a compact oval of pale grey merino, approximately 3 cm across. Felt it separately until quite firm. Attach to the front end of the body by felting through the join from multiple angles. The grey head should sit slightly forward of the body axis, giving the kite its characteristic lean-forward profile.
Step 4: wing panels. Lay the pre-felted white sheet flat on your foam pad. The wing crossbar of your
armature

sits along the centre line of the sheet. Felt the wire to the sheet by pressing roving over it and stabbing through. Once the wire is held, build the wing shape outward: broad at the body, tapered at the tip, with a slight backward sweep. Each wing panel should be roughly 12 cm x 6 cm at its widest point.
Step 5: wing surface colour. Using
wool tops

in rust, dark brown, and cream, layer the wing pattern. Red kite wings are reddish-brown on the upper surface with pale streaks. The underside shows a large cream or buff patch with brown wingtip and trailing-edge contrast. Work the upper surface first, then turn the panel over and work the underside. Use a 42-gauge needle for the fine streak lines.

Step 6: forked tail. The tail is worked separately on the foam pad as a flat panel roughly 7 cm long with a pronounced V-notch at the tip. Use reddish-brown wool tops. Felt the panel to medium density, cut the fork with sharp scissors, then felt the cut edges to prevent fraying. Attach the tail to the rear of the body by felting in along the base.

Step 7: surface detail on body. Cover the body with thin layers of rust and reddish-brown wool tops. Add cream-coloured streaks along the breast and upper belly using very fine wisps of cream tops, felted in with a 42-gauge needle. The pale grey head should show fine dark streaking: add these using a single needle and very small wisps of dark brown tops.

Step 8: beak and eye. Roll a small cone of yellow wool tops for the beak, felt firm, then curve the tip slightly downward before attaching. The eye is a 5 mm yellow or amber plastic safety eye. Press the post through from outside, push the washer on from behind the head with a blunt darning needle.

Step 9: legs and feet. Cut two 6 cm lengths of wire, bend each into a foot shape with three forward toes and one back toe. Wrap in yellow roving. Felt the leg wires up into the base of the body. The kite is usually displayed as a mounted piece rather than free-standing; bend the feet around a twig or dowel and felt in at the grip point to hold the pose.

items.cause: The felt is too thin relative to the wire span
items.fix: Add more roving to the panel, felting both sides evenly until the panel holds a flat plane. Pin it to a blocking board while drying if the foam is inadequate.

items.symptom: Wire showing through the surface colour
items.cause: The roving wrap around the wire was too thin
items.fix: Add wisps of matching roving over the exposed wire and felt firmly downward using a 38-gauge needle until covered.

items.symptom: Colour streaks look blotchy rather than fine
items.cause: Wisps of wool tops were too thick
items.fix: Pull each wisp finer before placing. Use a 42-gauge needle and work in one direction only for each streak.

items.cause: The head oval was built up too much before checking proportion
items.fix: You cannot remove felt once it is in, but you can reshape by felting from the sides to compress the head slightly. For a severe size mismatch, build a new head.

content.text: Mounting and display

The red kite is best displayed mounted on a branch or piece of driftwood with its wings slightly open. Secure the feet to the branch by wrapping a length of matching roving around the grip point and felting in. A wall-mounted branch at eye level shows the wingspan to best effect.
The
barbs

on felting needles blunt faster when working through thick layers. Replace your working needle as soon as it starts dragging rather than sinking cleanly. Keep the piece out of direct sunlight to preserve the rust and cream colour tones in the
wool tops
